Restriction (and then sealing) is available along several pathways. The agent routes by disposition first.
Non-conviction records (the core path, § 35-3-37(h))
Misdemeanor convictions under SB 288 (§ 35-3-37(j)(4))
Since January 1, 2021, a person may petition the court to restrict and seal up to two misdemeanor convictions
in a lifetime, provided all sentence terms are complete, the person has no convictions for at least four years
before filing, has no pending charges, and the offense is not on the excluded list (Section 3). Grant is
discretionary on an interests-of-justice showing.
Pardoned felonies (§ 35-3-37(j)(7))
A felony conviction that has been pardoned by the Georgia State Board of Pardons and Paroles may be
restricted if the person has not been convicted of a new offense (other than a non-serious traffic offense) since
the pardon and has no pending charges. This is one of the few routes to restrict a felony conviction.

exclusionRules: For SB 288 misdemeanor-conviction restriction, § 35-3-37(j)(4) excludes a specific list of misdemeanors. A
conviction for any of the following generally cannot be restricted, regardless of time elapsed:

Family violence simple assault, simple battery, and battery under §§ 16-5-20(d), 16-5-23(f), 16-5-23.1(f) — unless the person was under 21 at the time of the arrest.
Theft offenses under Chapter 8 of Title 16 — except theft by shoplifting and refund fraud, which can be restricted.
Sexual offenses, including sexual battery, public indecency, and related crimes.
Crimes against children / family, including certain child-related and exploitation offenses.
Two-misdemeanor lifetime cap. No more than two misdemeanor convictions may ever be restricted under SB 288.
Felony convictions generally cannot be restricted except through a pardon (§ 35-3-37(j)(7)) or retroactive first-offender treatment.
Pending charges / new convictions disqualify, and a new conviction can undo eligibility and reset the four-year clock.

Out-of-state / federal records are not reachable by Georgia restriction.

pathways.label: SB 288 misdemeanor-conviction restriction and sealing
pathways.summary: 1 Confirm eligibility: sentence complete, four conviction-free years, no pending charges, not an excluded offense, and within the two-misdemeanor lifetime cap. 2 Prepare a Petition for Record Restriction and Sealing citing § 35-3-37(j)(4), attaching the GCIC history and proof of completion, plus a proposed order. 3 File in the Superior/State Court of the county of conviction and pay the filing fee (county-set).
pathways.sourceRef: section:273-294; pathways:68-115; section:68-115; section:116-150; corpus-unit:67; waiting_periods:116-150; corpus-unit:32; corpus-unit:57
ruleClauses: 1 Confirm eligibility: sentence complete, four conviction-free years, no pending charges, not an excluded
offense, and within the two-misdemeanor lifetime cap.
2 Prepare a Petition for Record Restriction and Sealing citing § 35-3-37(j)(4), attaching the GCIC history and
proof of completion, plus a proposed order.
3 File in the Superior/State Court of the county of conviction and pay the filing fee (county-set).
4 Serve the prosecuting attorney, who has 90 days to object; if unopposed, the court may grant on the papers,
otherwise it holds an interests-of-justice hearing.
5 If granted, the court orders GCIC and the arresting agency to restrict; then file the separate § 35-3-37(m)
motion to seal the clerk's court file.
Identify the arrest date early — the July 1, 2013 line decides whether non-conviction restriction is
automatic or application-based.
For convictions, remember it is a two-step finish: court-ordered restriction, then a separate motion to seal
the clerk's file. Don't stop at restriction if the user wants the court record closed too.

Each court petition is accompanied by a proposed Order reciting the caption and directing the Georgia Crime

the Clerk of Court to seal the case file under § 35-3-37(m).

ruleClauses: Misdemeanors committed before age 21 (with exceptions for serious traffic, sexual, and certain other offenses)
have long been restrictable, and retroactive First Offender treatment (O.C.G.A. § 42-8-66) can in some cases

The separate sealing step (§ 35-3-37(m))
Restriction hides the record in the GCIC criminal-history system, but the clerk of court's file remains a public
court record. To close that, the person files a motion/petition to seal the clerk's record under § 35-3-37(m).
Restriction must occur first; sealing is the second step.

sourceSections.title: Eligibility rules
sourceSections.text:
Restriction (and then sealing) is available along several pathways. The agent routes by disposition first.

Pathway 1 — Non-conviction records (the core path, § 35-3-37(h))
Charges that did not result in a conviction are the primary eligible category: dismissed, nolle prossed/prosequi,
not presented to the grand jury, no further action anticipated, dead docketed (for the required period), acquittal,
or completion of a pretrial diversion program. Whether action is required depends on the arrest date:

• Arrests on or after July 1, 2013: generally restricted automatically when the clerk or prosecutor enters the
qualifying disposition into the GCIC database — no application process; the person may contact the
prosecutor to confirm.
• Arrests before July 1, 2013: the person must apply for restriction through the arresting agency using the
GBI Request to Restrict Arrest Record form, with prosecutor approval.
• Automatic restriction (§ 35-3-37(j.1), 2021): certain non-conviction records are restricted automatically
without any request.

Pathway 2 — Misdemeanor convictions under SB 288 (§ 35-3-37(j)(4))
Since January 1, 2021, a person may petition the court to restrict and seal up to two misdemeanor convictions
in a lifetime, provided all sentence terms are complete, the person has no convictions for at least four years
before filing, has no pending charges, and the offense is not on the excluded list (Section 3). Grant is
discretionary on an interests-of-justice showing.

Pathway 3 — Pardoned felonies (§ 35-3-37(j)(7))
A felony conviction that has been pardoned by the Georgia State Board of Pardons and Paroles may be
restricted if the person has not been convicted of a new offense (other than a non-serious traffic offense) since
the pardon and has no pending charges. This is one of the few routes to restrict a felony conviction.

Pathway 4 — Youthful / first-offender and related routes
Misdemeanors committed before age 21 (with exceptions for serious traffic, sexual, and certain other offenses)
have long been restrictable, and retroactive First Offender treatment (O.C.G.A. § 42-8-66) can in some cases
convert a conviction into a first-offender discharge that is then restrictable.

The separate sealing step (§ 35-3-37(m))
Restriction hides the record in the GCIC criminal-history system, but the clerk of court's file remains a public
court record. To close that, the person files a motion/petition to seal the clerk's record under § 35-3-37(m).
Restriction must occur first; sealing is the second step.

Georgia Record Restriction & Sealing — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

AGENT GUIDANCE
Route by disposition: non-conviction → restriction (automatic post-2013, or by application pre-2013);
misdemeanor conviction → SB 288 petition (mind the two-in-a-lifetime cap); felony → generally only via
pardon, then § 35-3-37(j)(7).
Always treat sealing of the court file as a distinct second step. Telling a user their record is 'restricted' is
not the same as the court file being sealed — explain both.

Georgia Record Restriction & Sealing — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

Misdemeanor before age 21 Restrict Per statute Exceptions for serious offenses

The SB 288 conditions stack: a person must (1) have completed all terms of the sentence; (2) have no
convictions for at least four years before filing; and (3) have no pending charges. The four-year window is
measured backward from the filing date and is broken by any intervening conviction, so a new offense resets the
clock. The two-misdemeanor cap is a lifetime limit, not per-filing — a person who has already restricted two
misdemeanor convictions cannot restrict a third.

AGENT GUIDANCE
For SB 288, confirm sentence completion (jail done AND probation terminated), four conviction-free
years, and no pending charges before indicating eligibility. A later conviction resets the four-year clock.
Track the lifetime two-misdemeanor cap; ask whether the user has previously restricted any conviction
before promising a new one is possible.

sourceSections.title: Disqualifying / excluded offenses
sourceSections.text:
For SB 288 misdemeanor-conviction restriction, § 35-3-37(j)(4) excludes a specific list of misdemeanors. A
conviction for any of the following generally cannot be restricted, regardless of time elapsed:

• DUI and all other serious traffic offenses under O.C.G.A. §§ 40-6-390 through 40-6-397 (e.g., reckless
driving, fleeing).
• Family violence simple assault, simple battery, and battery under §§ 16-5-20(d), 16-5-23(f), 16-5-23.1(f) —
unless the person was under 21 at the time of the arrest.
• Theft offenses under Chapter 8 of Title 16 — except theft by shoplifting and refund fraud, which can be
restricted.
• Sexual offenses, including sexual battery, public indecency, and related crimes.
• Crimes against children / family, including certain child-related and exploitation offenses.
• Offenses involving serious injury or endangerment as enumerated in the statute.

Other categorical limits
• Two-misdemeanor lifetime cap. No more than two misdemeanor convictions may ever be restricted under
SB 288.
• Felony convictions generally cannot be restricted except through a pardon (§ 35-3-37(j)(7)) or retroactive
first-offender treatment.
• Pending charges / new convictions disqualify, and a new conviction can undo eligibility and reset the
four-year clock.
• Plea-bargain dismissals. A charge dismissed as part of a plea to another charge arising from the same
conduct is not restrictable.
• Out-of-state / federal records are not reachable by Georgia restriction.

VERIFY BEFORE RELYING
The § 35-3-37(j)(4) excluded list is specific and the family-violence and theft carve-outs (under-21
exception; shoplifting/refund-fraud inclusion) turn on the exact O.C.G.A. section and facts. The
agent must map the user's actual offense to its Code section and verify against the current
excluded list before declaring a misdemeanor eligible — the offense label alone is not enough.

AGENT GUIDANCE
DUI and theft are the two most common disqualifiers users misjudge: DUI is categorically excluded, and
most theft is excluded EXCEPT shoplifting and refund fraud. Check the precise offense.
For family-violence misdemeanors, the under-21-at-arrest exception is the only opening — confirm age
at arrest before ruling it in or out.

sourceSections.title: Case outcome rules
sourceSections.text:
Disposition determines the pathway, whether action is needed, and who decides. This table is the agent's
primary routing logic for Georgia.

Case outcome Pathway Route / who decides Condition

Dismissed / nolle prosequi § 35-3-37(h) Automatic via GCIC entry Qualifying disposition entered
/ acquitted (≥ 7/1/2013)

Non-conviction § 35-3-37(h) Apply at arresting agency; GBI Request to Restrict form
(arrest < 7/1/2013) prosecutor approves

Auto-restriction category § 35-3-37(j.1) Automatic, no request Statute-specified records

Pretrial diversion done § 35-3-37(h) Restriction on completion Program completed

Misdemeanor conviction § 35-3-37(j)(4) Petition to court; 4 yrs; ≤ 2 lifetime; eligible
prosecutor 90-day objection

Pardoned felony § 35-3-37(j)(7) Petition to court No new offense since pardon

Excluded offense (Sec. 3) — Not restrictable Pardon / first-offender only

On a SB 288 petition, the prosecuting attorney has 90 days to object; if there is no objection the court may grant
without a hearing, and if the prosecutor objects the court holds an interests-of-justice hearing. For
non-conviction applications denied by the prosecutor, the person may appeal to the Superior Court within 30
days. The effect of relief: a restricted record does not appear on a GCIC background check seen by employers,
landlords, or the general public, and the person may state the record does not exist; but criminal justice agencies
retain access, and the clerk's court file stays public until separately sealed under § 35-3-37(m).

AGENT GUIDANCE
Tell users plainly: restriction hides the record from background checks but does not erase it, and the
court file remains public until sealed in the separate § 35-3-37(m) step.
Flag the decision-maker: non-conviction restriction runs through the prosecutor/GCIC; SB 288 conviction
restriction runs through the court with a 90-day prosecutor objection window.

Georgia Record Restriction & Sealing — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Required forms
sourceSections.text:
Georgia uses a mix of a GBI agency form (for non-conviction restriction) and court petitions (for SB 288
conviction restriction and for sealing). The agent picks by pathway.

Non-conviction restriction — GBI form
• Request to Restrict Arrest Record — the GBI/GCIC application (used mainly for pre-July-2013 arrests, or
to correct a record that was not auto-restricted). Section Three is completed by the prosecutor.
• For post-July-2013 arrests, no form is needed if the qualifying disposition was entered into GCIC; the person
contacts the prosecutor to confirm.

SB 288 misdemeanor-conviction restriction — court petition
• Petition for Record Restriction and Sealing filed in the Superior (or State) Court of the county of
conviction, citing § 35-3-37(j)(4), with a proposed order.
• Attach the GCIC criminal history and proof that the sentence is complete and the four-year window is
satisfied.
• Serve the prosecuting attorney, who has 90 days to object.

Sealing the clerk's file — separate motion
• Motion/Petition to Seal Court Records under § 35-3-37(m), filed after restriction, asking the court to seal
the clerk of court's file with a proposed sealing order.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Pick by pathway: GBI Request to Restrict for non-conviction (esp. pre-2013); a § 35-3-37(j)(4) court
petition for SB 288 misdemeanors; a § 35-3-37(m) motion to seal the court file as the second step.
Always attach a current GCIC criminal history to a court petition, and pair each petition with a proposed
order the judge can sign.

Georgia Record Restriction & Sealing — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Non-conviction restriction (agency/prosecutor process)
sourceSections.text:
1 Obtain and review your GCIC criminal history to confirm each charge has a final, qualifying disposition.
2 If the arrest was before July 1, 2013, get the Request to Restrict Arrest Record form from the arresting
agency; if on/after July 1, 2013, contact the prosecutor to confirm automatic restriction.

3 Submit the application to the arresting agency / prosecutor; the prosecutor completes Section Three (approve
or deny).

4 If approved, the prosecutor enters the restriction code in the GCIC CCH interface, or the approved application
plus the GCIC processing fee is forwarded to GCIC.
5 GCIC processes complete applications (typically 2–3 weeks) and mails a completion letter. If the prosecutor
denies, appeal to the Superior Court within 30 days.

sourceSections.title: SB 288 conviction restriction (court process)
sourceSections.text:
1 Confirm eligibility: sentence complete, four conviction-free years, no pending charges, not an excluded
offense, and within the two-misdemeanor lifetime cap.

2 Prepare a Petition for Record Restriction and Sealing citing § 35-3-37(j)(4), attaching the GCIC history and
proof of completion, plus a proposed order.

3 File in the Superior/State Court of the county of conviction and pay the filing fee (county-set).
4 Serve the prosecuting attorney, who has 90 days to object; if unopposed, the court may grant on the papers,
otherwise it holds an interests-of-justice hearing.

5 If granted, the court orders GCIC and the arresting agency to restrict; then file the separate § 35-3-37(m)
motion to seal the clerk's court file.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Identify the arrest date early — the July 1, 2013 line decides whether non-conviction restriction is
automatic or application-based.
For convictions, remember it is a two-step finish: court-ordered restriction, then a separate motion to seal
the clerk's file. Don't stop at restriction if the user wants the court record closed too.

sourceSections.title: Fee information
sourceSections.text:
Item Amount Note

GCIC restriction processing fee Set by GCIC Per application; payable to GBI by money order/certified check

Automatic restriction (≥ 7/1/2013) $0 No application, no fee

SB 288 court petition filing fee ~$50–$250 Varies by county; per petition

GCIC criminal history report Fee set by GCIC To review the record before applying

Attorney fees (if represented) Varies Not required; Georgia Justice Project offers free help to eligible people

Board of Pardons application Varies Only for the pardon route to a felony

The cost depends entirely on pathway. Automatic non-conviction restriction (post-July-2013) costs nothing. A
pre-2013 non-conviction application carries the GCIC processing fee. An SB 288 conviction petition carries a
county-set court filing fee, generally in the $50–$250 range. The Georgia Justice Project provides free
representation to eligible people who cannot afford an attorney.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Lead with the free path: if a non-conviction arrest is post-July-2013, restriction should be automatic at no
cost — the user mainly needs to confirm via their GCIC history.
Quote SB 288 petition fees as a county-dependent range, and point low-income users to the Georgia
Justice Project (404-827-... helpline) for free help.

sourceSections.text:
A 2020 charge dismissed/nolle prossed is restricted
automatically when the clerk or prosecutor enters the
disposition into GCIC. No application needed; confirm via the
GCIC criminal history. A 35-3-37(m) sealing motion can still
be filed to close the clerk's court file.

sourceSections.title: PARDONED FELONY (§ 35-3-37(j)(7)):
sourceSections.text:
A felony conviction pardoned by the State Board of Pardons and
Paroles may be restricted by petition if there is no new
offense since the pardon and no pending charges — one of the
few routes to restrict a Georgia felony conviction.

Proposed Order (court pathways)
Each court petition is accompanied by a proposed Order reciting the caption and directing the Georgia Crime
Information Center and the arresting agency to restrict the record under § 35-3-37, and, where sought, ordering
the Clerk of Court to seal the case file under § 35-3-37(m).

This reference is an internal training and retrieval aid for the Wilma assistant. It summarizes Georgia statutes and common practice
the current text of O.C.G.A. § 35-3-37, the § (j)(4) excluded-offense list, GCIC request procedures, county filing specifics, and the
two-step restriction-then-sealing process before relying on any rule here.
